entry.title: Analyze single cell multi-modal data using Seurat V4 and MOFA: Part I

link.title: Analyze single cell multi-modal data using Seurat V4 and MOFA: Part I

entry.content:
Recent advances in technology has enabled measuring information of multiple layers from a single cell, resulting in so-called single cell multi-omic data, such as SHARE-seq or Multiome, where the chromatin accessibility and gene expression from the exact same cells are quantified. Given this kind of data, an interesting question is how to computational analyze them. In particular, how to perform dimensionality reduction, how to define the clusters and visualize the data, given the fact that each modality captures different information and they have very different property in terms of the readout.

Two methods were developed for this task, namely Seurat V4 and MOFA+. In this blog series, I will use them to analyze a single cell multi-omic data generated using 10x multiome protocol. The raw data can be found here and I will use the processed R object from MOFA+ tutorial.

Let’s first go through the method to understand the basic idea. I will begin with Seurat V4 because I have used the previous version of Seurat a lot in my projects and I am more familiar with it.

The core problem when processing multi-mode data is how to determine the similarity of cells, i.e., how to combine the distances estimated from multiple data types. Seurat V4 tried to solve this problem using weighted nearest neighbor analysis. Specifically, given K data types and any two cells i and j, it estimates the similarity as follows:

This formula reminds me of another paper, SIMLR, which uses multiple kernel learning to learn a cell-cell similarity matrix, as shown below.
entry.summary: Recent advances in technology has enabled measuring information of multiple layers from a single cell, resulting in so-called single cell multi-omic data, such as SHARE-seq or Multiome, where the chromatin accessibility and gene expression from the exact same cells are quantified. Given this kind of data, an interesting question is how to computational analyze them. In particular, how to perform dimensionality reduction, how to define the clusters and visualize the data, given the fact that each modality captures different information and they have very different property in terms of the readout.
